steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The student must make a generalization about exoplanets as a category and support it with evidence from the notes. The decisive clue is that both Phobetor (Greek mythology) and Mastika (Malay) appear in the notes with their naming origins explained.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Exoplanets that are named Phobetor orbit Lich, and those that are named Mastika orbit Gumala.': Misrepresents the notes by implying multiple exoplanets share each name, and states an orbit fact rather than forming any generalization. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Even though Phobetor and Mastika are both exoplanets, their masses are different...': Contrasts two specific exoplanets on mass; describes individual facts, not a broader claim about the category. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Many stars have both a designation and a proper name...': Makes a generalization about stars, not exoplanets, so it targets the wrong subject. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Exoplanet names have diverse origins, a fact that can be seen in the cases of Phobetor...and Mastika...': States a generalization about all exoplanets (diverse naming origins) and backs it with both examples from the notes. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
